design_philosophy.core_loop: Select character → fight sequential opponents across 15 matches → build momentum through wins → face final boss (Masked Warrior)
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Attrition-based combat where player must deplete opponent health while managing crowd interference and environmental hazards
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Raw underground fighter working up through a tournament bracket, leveraging personal fighting style (wrestler strength, kickboxer speed, or martial artist technique) against diverse opponents
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Digitized live-action sprite technology prioritizes authentic martial arts movement over stylized animation; three-character roster with distinct archetypes forces playstyle commitment; interactive crowd creates unpredictable match conditions rather than purely skill-based duels

difficulty.lives_system: Three knockdowns eliminate player in Grudge Match bonus rounds; knockout results in loss of match
difficulty.checkpointing: 15 sequential matches with Grudge Match interruptions every third fight; progression resets on tournament loss
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Character selection at start determines speed/power tradeoff (wrestler vs kickboxer vs martial artist); three-button combo for 'super move' provides skilled players advantage

progression.upgrade_system: Power pill temporary power-up appears during matches; bonus money earned from Grudge Match victories (mechanical rather than persistent upgrade)
progression.unlock_mechanism: 15 fixed matches unlock sequentially; Masked Warrior final boss appears only after completing 15-match gauntlet; multiplayer modes available from start
replayability_hooks: Three distinct playable characters with different move sets and speed/power profiles
Crowd interference varies each playthrough (non-deterministic environmental threats)
Multiplayer Grudge Matches escalate final tournament stage (PvP instead of CPU clone)
Multiple opponent roster (8 named fighters across 15 matches implies repeating matchups with variation)

world_building_techniques: Digitized live-action actors ground fighting game in pseudo-realistic martial arts aesthetic
Character archetypes (ex-professional wrestler, kickboxing champion, black belt) communicated through visual design and performance
Mysterious Masked Warrior antagonist taunts between matches, building anticipation for final confrontation
Interactive crowd (attacking, dropping weapons, pushing fighters) creates tournament atmosphere without explicit narrative exposition

boss_design.philosophy: Masked Warrior serves as narrative culmination and final skill gate; represents unknown opponent that has haunted the fighter throughout tournament progression
notable_bosses: Masked Warrior (final championship match opponent; mysterious taunting entity throughout tournament)

faction_indicators: Buzz archetype: American wrestler (strength-based, traditional power fighter)
Kato archetype: Japanese-American martial artist (quick strikes, precision)
Opponent roster suggests underground fighting circuit crossing ethnic and regional boundaries
danger_signals: Crowd interference as match hazard (unpredictable attack/weapon drops/pushing)
Chainman Eddie and other weapon-wielding opponents introduce external threat beyond pure martial skill
Executioner and Southside Jim character names evoke violence outside sporting context
environmental_storytelling: Interactive crowd presence transforms arena into chaotic rather than controlled sports environment
Digitized real-actor aesthetic grounds fighting in pseudo-realistic underground tournament setting
Progression through 15 matches builds sense of rising stakes and difficulty as fighter advances

sources_used: wikipedia (Pit-Fighter article: gameplay mechanics, character roster, releases, design methodology)
